steps.title: Reach out to a nonprofit credit counselor about the $2,000 payday loan
steps.description: Contact a nonprofit credit counseling agency (look for NFCC-member organizations) to explore hardship programs or payday loan relief options. At 35% APR, this $2,000 loan grows by roughly $58 every month you can't pay it.
steps.impact: A nonprofit counselor may be able to negotiate a payment pause, reduced rate, or hardship plan — stopping the $58/mo interest bleed before it compounds further.

steps.title: Apply for emergency assistance to cover the $500/mo in expenses
steps.description: Contact local social services, 211 (dial 2-1-1), food banks, and community organizations. With $0 income and $500/mo in expenses, outside support isn't optional right now — it's the bridge.
steps.impact: Covering basic expenses through assistance programs frees any future income to go toward the payday loan rather than just survival.

steps.title: Identify any income source — even $200-$400/mo changes everything
steps.description: Explore gig work, day labor, selling unused items, or temporary work agencies. Even $200-$400/mo in income begins to shift the math from 'compounding crisis' to 'manageable problem.'
steps.impact: At $400/mo income, you could cover the $200 minimum payment and $200 toward expenses — stopping the debt from growing and stabilizing your situation.

steps.title: Explore payday loan alternatives to escape the 35% APR trap
steps.description: Ask your credit counselor about credit union payday alternative loans (PALs), which cap interest around 28%. Refinancing the $2,000 at even 20% lower APR saves roughly $350 over the repayment period.
steps.impact: Replacing a 35% APR loan with a lower-rate alternative could save $350+ and reduce the monthly payment burden while income is being rebuilt.

steps.title: Once income starts, direct every extra dollar above $500 to the payday loan
steps.description: As soon as any income arrives, cover your $500/mo in expenses first, then put everything remaining toward the $2,000 balance. At $700/mo income, that's $200/mo toward the loan — paid off in about 11 months.
steps.impact: Eliminating the $2,000 payday loan removes $200/mo in minimum payments and ends the 35% APR drain — freeing cash flow for the first time.

steps.title: Review your situation and build even a $500 emergency buffer
steps.description: By week 12, take stock: Is income stable? Is the loan balance shrinking? Even saving $500 — one month of expenses — breaks the cycle of needing high-interest loans when something goes wrong.
steps.impact: A $500 buffer means the next unexpected $200 expense doesn't send you back to a payday lender. It's a small number with an outsized psychological and financial impact.

rawResponseBody.overallMessage: This is a genuinely hard place to be, and it makes sense that it feels overwhelming. The numbers here — $0 income, $500/mo in expenses, a $2,000 loan at 35% — don't leave much room for traditional financial advice. The first 30 days aren't about budgeting. They're about stabilizing: finding support, stopping the loan from growing, and locating any income at all. Every step here is marked low-confi
